title: Import flowtools.directory into ListHub
description:
Import the flowtools.directory content into ListHub. Flowtools.directory is a curated directory of tools for thought / knowledge management / PKM tools.

Steps:
- Scrape or export the directory entries from flowtools.directory
- Import them as ListHub items with structured metadata (tool name, URL, description, category, tags)
- Could become a showcase for ListHub's directory view feature (listhub-ogp)

This is linked to listhub-ogp (the publishable curated directory UI) — the flowtools import would serve as the first real content powering that directory view. Also linked to listhub-bd1 (view templates) for how the imported data gets displayed.

title: Activity pulse / recent updates view
description:
## Goal
Show a pulse/activity feed of recent updates across ListHub — what's been created, edited, who's active. Makes the site feel alive and helps users discover new content.

## Ideas to Explore

### Activity Feed (GitHub-style)
- Chronological feed of recent public activity: "alice created Best AI Tools", "bob updated Reading List 2026"
- Could live at /activity or /pulse as its own page
- Or embedded as a section on the homepage or people page

### Per-User Activity
- On each user's profile: show recent activity (created X, updated Y)
- Helps visitors understand what someone is actively working on
- Already have `last_active` from MAX(updated_at) — could show more granular timeline

### Pulse Dashboard
- GitHub Insights-style overview: how many lists created this week, active contributors, trending lists
- Could show graphs of activity over time
- Most edited lists (high revision counts = actively maintained)

### People Page Integration
- The people page now sorts by "Recently Active" — could show more context about *what* they were active on
- Mini activity snippet: "last updated: AI Tools list (2 hours ago)"

## Implementation Notes
- All data already exists (item.updated_at, item.created_at, item_version for edit history)
- No new tables needed — just queries and a new template
- Could start simple: a page that shows the last 50 public item creates/edits with timestamps and usernames
- item_version table has per-edit history if we want detailed "what changed" view

title: Agent-friendly search surface across all ListHub items
description:
## Question to think about first
**Does agent search even make sense as a dedicated feature, or should we just expose git-clone/list-items and let the agent walk the tree?**

This is the fundamental design question. There are two coherent models:

### Model A: Filesystem-first (the "agents are coding agents" view)
- Agents clone the repo (git clone over HTTPS with API key)
- They walk directories, grep markdown, read files directly
- No dedicated search endpoint needed — git + grep is the interface
- This matches how Claude Code works with any codebase
- Pros: simple, agent-friendly in a boring way, no new API surface
- Cons: requires clone (large repos slow), no relevance ranking, no cross-user search

### Model B: API-first (the "agents are apps" view)
- Agents call `GET /api/v1/search?q=...` and get structured, ranked results
- Server does FTS5 relevance ranking, visibility filtering, pagination
- No clone needed; works for agents that don't have filesystem access
- Pros: fast, remote-friendly, doesn't require git
- Cons: we own a search index that needs to stay correct; another endpoint to maintain

### Probably: both
- `GET /api/v1/search` already exists for Model B (keyword FTS5, owner-scoped)
- Add `scope=public|mine|all` parameter to expose cross-user search
- Add `GET /api/v1/search/semantic?q=...` later for embeddings-based retrieval
- Keep git-clone access as the "walk everything" backup
- Document both in llms.txt so agents know their options

## Concrete improvements to existing search

Current state: `GET /api/v1/search?q=X` returns items **owned by the authenticated user only**. For an agent to find public content across the whole site, it doesn't work.

Changes needed:
1. **Add `scope` parameter**: `mine` (default, current behavior), `public` (only public items, no auth required), `all` (authenticated user's items + all public)
2. **Include `owner_username` in results** so agents can construct URLs
3. **Include a `matched_snippet` field** with FTS5 highlight so the agent sees why something matched
4. **Pagination**: `page` and `per_page` query params (current returns up to 100)
5. **Filter by type/tag/visibility** combinators in one call
6. **Response shape stable and versioned**: document the schema in llms.txt

## Document agent search in llms.txt

The current llms.txt mentions `GET /api/v1/search?q=:query` but doesn't explain:
- That it's owner-scoped (big gotcha for agents looking for public content)
- How to clone the git repo as an alternative
- When to prefer which

Update llms.txt with a "How to find things" section that explicitly discusses both paths.

## Semantic search (future)

FTS5 keyword search is fine for developer-style agents that know the exact terms. For natural-language agents ("find me that note about Dr. Alderman and PRP injections"), embeddings-based semantic search is dramatically better.

Plan:
- Generate OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` vectors for each item on write
- Store in a `item_embedding` table (SQLite blob, or an actual vector DB later)
- Add `GET /api/v1/search/semantic?q=...` that does cosine similarity
- Fall back to FTS5 if embeddings aren't available

Defer this to V2. V1 = fix the scope bug and document the existing endpoint properly.

## Related
- `listhub-95u` — Global search UI (uses this API underneath)
- `listhub-3wv` — Search-or-create (layered on top)
- Existing `GET /api/v1/search` in `api.py`
- `llms.txt` discovery doc (needs updating)

title: Move visibility/edit permissions controls to top of list page
description:
## Problem
Currently, visibility and edit permissions (private/public/public_edit/shared) are only accessible in the edit form, buried below the content editor. When viewing your own list, there's no quick way to toggle permissions without going into full edit mode.

## Design Goals
- As the list owner, I should see visibility/permissions controls at the top of the list view page
- One-click toggle between private, public (view only), and public (anyone can edit)
- Should feel lightweight — not a full settings panel, more like a quick dropdown or segmented control
- Non-owners should see a read-only badge showing current visibility (already partially there with badges)

## Possible Designs

### Option A: Inline dropdown in header
Next to the list title on /@username/slug, show a small dropdown:
`[🔒 Private ▾]` → click → `Private | Public | Public Edit | Shared (WIP)`
Saves immediately via API call, no page reload needed.

### Option B: Segmented control bar
A thin bar below the title with toggle buttons:
`🔒 Private | 🌐 Public | ✏️ Anyone Can Edit`
Active state highlighted. Click to switch.

### Option C: Settings gear icon
Small ⚙️ icon next to title that expands a mini-panel with:
- Visibility toggle
- Share settings
- Slug/URL display
- Edit button

## Scope
- Start with owner's view only (when viewing your own list at /@you/slug)
- Later: could extend to dashboard list view (quick toggle per list row)
- API already supports `PUT /api/v1/items/:id` with visibility field — just need the frontend

title: Import curated noos knowledge documents into ListHub
description:
## Goal
Import curated knowledge documents from Noos into ListHub as publicly browsable items.

## Source
Noos (globalbr.ai) is Jacob's knowledge graph platform. It has accumulated curated knowledge documents that would benefit from being publicly browsable on ListHub.

## What to Import
- Knowledge map documents (AI/ML curriculum, learning paths)
- Curated research notes and insights
- Any public-facing knowledge that's currently only accessible via the Noos API

## Implementation
- Use the noos CLI to query public nodes
- Convert to ListHub items via the API
- Preserve tags and metadata
- Link back to Noos node IDs for reference

## Related
- The Moltbook knowledge docs in noos/knowledge/ are covered by listhub-tdb
- This ticket covers OTHER noos content beyond Moltbook

title: Agent self-service onboarding (signup, API key, MCP/skill)
description: Make it trivial for AI agents to onboard themselves. 1) Agent self-registration API endpoint (POST /api/v1/auth/register). 2) Agent API key provisioning via Bearer token (not just session). 3) Agent discovery: llms.txt, MCP server, Claude Code skill, OpenAPI spec. 4) Documentation: agent onboarding as THE first thing in API docs. DISCUSS BEFORE IMPLEMENTING — security implications.
close_reason: Implemented: POST /api/v1/auth/register for programmatic account creation, llms.txt, agent quick start on landing page, registration docs in API page

title: Git history viewer for user repos
description:
Add a way to see the git history of each users items — what changed, when, diffs. Options considered:

1. Roll our own: render git log + diffs in the web UI. Simplest, full control. Could use pygit2 or shell out to git commands on bare repos. Show on /@user/item page as a "History" tab.

2. Use Forgejo/Gitea: self-hosted GitHub clone that can display repos. Heavyweight but gets you PRs, issues, web editor for free. Overkill for this use case.

3. Auto-push to GitHub: Mirror each users bare repo to a GitHub repo (e.g. listhub-USERNAME). Gets GitHub UI for free. BUT: user repos contain ALL their items including private ones — would need to filter. Also creates GitHub repo management overhead.

RECOMMENDATION: Roll our own minimal history viewer. The bare repos already exist. Add a /git/@username/history route that shows git log, and /git/@username/item/diff/:sha for individual diffs. Private repos should only be viewable by the owner (auth check). Public items history could be visible to anyone.

Key question: repos are per-user (not per-item) and contain ALL items. So viewing others repos means seeing their full file list. Need to filter to only show public items in the history view for non-owners.

title: Auto-push user repos to GitHub + encrypted items architecture
description:
## Goal
Automatically mirror ListHub user repositories (especially the community directory) to GitHub so content is easily browsable, forkable, and discoverable via GitHub search/UI.

## Motivation
- GitHub provides free browsing UI, markdown rendering, search, and PR-based contribution
- Community directories become much more accessible when on GitHub
- Git history already exists in ListHub — just need to push it upstream
- Related to listhub-j47 (git browsing on ListHub itself) but complementary

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Auto-push public repos to GitHub
- On each commit to a ListHub repo, push to a corresponding GitHub repo
- Could use GitHub org per ListHub user, or a single org with user-prefixed repos
- Trigger: post-commit hook or periodic cron sync
- Trade-offs: simple, great discoverability, but all content must be truly public

### Option B: Encrypted items within public repos
- Push repos to GitHub but encrypt specific line items or files marked as private/unlisted
- Uses age/GPG encryption per-item, with keys managed by ListHub
- Browsing on GitHub shows cleartext for public items, ciphertext blobs for private ones
- Trade-offs: more complex key management, but enables single repo with public+private

### Option C: Full encryption-first architecture
- ALL repos go to GitHub (one per user)
- Public items cleartext, private items encrypted at rest
- ListHub holds decryption keys and handles access control
- Supports encrypted line items as a first-class feature in the data model
- Trade-offs: most flexible but requires robust key management, rotation, sharing

## Trade-off Analysis
| Concern | Option A | Option B | Option C |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Discoverability | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Privacy | Public only | Mixed | Mixed |
| Key management | None | Per-item | Per-item + sharing |
| Git diff readability | Full | Partial | Partial |
| Contribution via PR | Easy | Awkward | Awkward |

## Recommendation
Start with Option A for community/public directories (quick win). Evaluate Option B/C for user repos with mixed visibility later. Encrypted items are valuable long-term but need careful design — item-level (content field) vs file-level (entire .md files).

## Open Questions
- Which GitHub org/account to push to?
- Opt-in vs default for public repos?
- For encrypted items: age vs GPG vs libsodium sealed boxes?
- How to handle PRs that touch encrypted items?

title: File bucket / upload architecture — decide where binary files live (Noos bucket vs local vs S3)
description:
## Goal
Decide where binary files (images, PDFs, audio, video) live for ListHub uploads. This is foundational for image support (listhub-c6x) and any future non-markdown content.

## Options

### Option A: Local disk under `LISTHUB_BLOB_ROOT`
- Pros: simple, no external dependency, fast, easy to back up with git or rsync
- Cons: doesnt scale to multiple app servers, prod disk needs to grow

### Option B: AWS S3 / Cloudflare R2
- Pros: scales, CDN-friendly, cheap at ListHub volumes
- Cons: one more service to manage, signed-URL complexity for private files, network hop latency

### Option C: Noos bucket
- Pros: reuses existing Noos infrastructure, aligns with the noos-as-knowledge-graph vision
- Cons: requires Noos to have file-storage primitives (does it today?), couples ListHub to Noos
- Question: is there an existing `noos` file bucket/blob API we can dogfood?

### Option D: IPFS / content-addressed distributed storage
- Pros: philosophically aligned with "Git-backed open knowledge" ethos, permanent
- Cons: operational complexity, retrieval latency, pinning cost

## Decision criteria
- How much is the user expected to upload in v1? (Probably MBs, not GBs.)
- Is ListHub expected to stay single-server for the foreseeable future?
- Do we want private files to require auth on every fetch?
- How important is dedup across users?

## Recommendation (to validate)
Start with **Option A (local disk)**, content-addressed by sha256. Migrate to R2/S3 when the disk fills up or when multi-server scaling becomes a need. Use signed URLs (HMAC with a secret) for private file access.

## Investigate first
- Does Noos already have a file bucket/blob API? If yes, prototype against it.
- What does Mew currently do for attachments?

## Related
- listhub-c6x — inline image support (blocked on this decision)
- listhub-4jm — multi-sheet CSV (related non-markdown content problem)

comments.author: Jacob Cole
comments.text:
## Codex second opinion: two-phase upload + auth at request time

Three operational requirements that MUST be in the blob storage design before implementation:

### 1. Two-phase upload (blob first, sidecar commit second)
You cannot transactionally couple a blob upload with a git commit. Failure modes:
- Blob uploads, sidecar never commits → orphaned blob
- Sidecar commits referencing missing blob → broken reference
- Force-push removes sidecar → orphaned blob

Required workflow:
1. Upload blob to content-addressed store, record sha256
2. Only then commit the sidecar referencing that sha256
3. Allow "pending" sidecars for git-first flows where blob is not yet uploaded

### 2. Reconciler / GC job
Periodic task that walks the blob store and all sidecar files, reports orphaned blobs and broken references, optionally GCs orphaned blobs after a grace period. Not optional.

### 3. Content-addressed storage is NOT access control
If blob URLs are /blobs/abc123.../ they are guessable. Visibility enforcement MUST happen at request time:
- Private blobs: signed URLs (HMAC, time-limited) OR session-auth-required
- Public blobs: still go through the app layer unless explicitly CDN-direct
- public_edit / shared / unlisted: session-auth required

### 4. Git-LFS vs separate blob store
Codex was even-handed. LFS is standard tooling but has platform limits. Separate blob store is more control but we own the integrity/GC/resumable-upload complexity. For ListHub's agent-first vision, separate blob store is recommended but the operational burden is real.

See listhub-r62.1 for full Codex critique.

comments.author: Jacob Cole
comments.text:

A **Codex critique** was added as a separate comment on this ticket on 2026-04-07. **Contrast the two before implementing** — some parts of my original proposal are now superseded by Codex's pushback.

**Key things to compare:**

comments.author: Jacob Cole
comments.text:
## 🔄 SCOPE NARROWED: blob store is for LARGE files only (2026-04-07)

Jacob pushed back on "all binaries in blob store" and the revised position is:

### Most binaries go directly in the git repo

Same as Obsidian, Hugo, Jekyll, Quartz. The git repo can handle PDFs, Word docs, images, audio clips up to ~25 MB each without any external storage. GitHub itself recommends ~1 GB max repo size, which accommodates hundreds of typical attachments.

### The blob store narrows to "large file offload"

This ticket's scope is now **only** for:

1. **Files > 25 MB** (configurable threshold) — offload to avoid repo bloat
2. **Content-addressed deduplication across users** (future feature) — "this PDF matches sha256 abc123 which user X has; store once, reference twice"
3. **Streaming / progressive downloads** — videos, audio where you want to stream without downloading the whole file
4. **Future file types** where size becomes a real issue

### What this means for the design

- **Most of the operational complexity disappears** for v1
- No need to worry about GC / orphan reconcilers for small files (they live in git, git tracks everything)
- Two-phase upload is still needed for the blob-store path, but only when a file exceeds the size threshold
- Content-addressed storage still applies for the blob store, just for the narrow case

### Revised decision criteria

| Aspect | Revised answer |
|---|---|
| Where do small binaries live? | **In the git repo** (Obsidian-style) |
| Where do large binaries live? | External blob store (this ticket) |
| What's "large"? | Default 25 MB, configurable per user |
| Git-LFS as alternative? | Yes, for users who prefer standard git tooling |
| Content-addressed? | Yes, for the blob store case only |
| Auth at request time? | Still required for the blob store case (Codex is right) |
| Two-phase upload? | Still required when offloading, not for small-in-git files |
| Reconciler / GC? | Only for the blob store, not for in-git files |

### Option comparison (updated)

| Approach | Fit |
|---|---|
| Local disk blob store under `LISTHUB_BLOB_ROOT` | **Best for v1** — simple, fast, works for the narrowed scope |
| S3 / R2 | Worth adding when we scale past single-server or need CDN delivery for large public files |
| Git-LFS | Alternative for users who prefer "standard git" — could be a per-user setting |
| Noos bucket | Still worth investigating for alignment with knowledge-graph ethos |
| IPFS | Philosophically interesting, defer |

### What this unblocks

With small binaries in git, we can ship inline image support (listhub-c6x) without waiting for the blob store decision. Just:
- Accept image uploads via `POST /api/v1/files` (small image path)
- Write the image file AND its sidecar to the users git repo
- Commit via the existing git sync pipeline
- The image gets a public URL at `/@user/attachments/foo.png` served directly from the git working copy

The blob store becomes a Phase 2 feature for large files only.

### Status of this ticket

Description is still the pre-Codex draft. This correction narrows the scope but doesnt change the underlying design for the large-file case. When implementing:
- Phase 1: small binaries in git, shipped with listhub-c6x and listhub-ma7
- Phase 2: large-file offload to a blob store (this ticket's revised scope)

Labels unchanged (still pre-codex-draft, needs-review).

title: Inline permissions and quick actions on item view
description:
## Problem
When viewing a list/item (not in edit mode), there's no way to quickly change its visibility or edit permissions. You have to click Edit, change the dropdown, and save — even if you just want to toggle between public and public_edit.

More broadly, the item viewing experience lacks inline controls that an owner (or authorized user) should have without entering full edit mode.

## Proposed Features

### 1. Inline visibility/permission toggle on item view
- Owner sees a clickable visibility badge on the item page (e.g., clicking "public" shows dropdown: private, public, public_edit)
- One click to change — no need to enter edit mode
- Same pattern as GitHub's repo visibility toggle

### 2. Quick actions on item cards (profile/dashboard/explore)
- Hover reveals action buttons: Edit, Share, Change Visibility
- Or: right-click context menu
- Or: "..." overflow menu on each card

### 3. Public edit items should show edit permission clearly
- When viewing a public_edit item as a non-owner, the "Edit" button should be prominent
- Show who last edited and how many contributors
- Consider: "N people have edited this" counter

### 4. Comprehensive UX audit for inline controls

**Owner viewing their own item should be able to:**
- [x] See current visibility (badge exists)
- [ ] Change visibility without entering edit mode
- [ ] Share with specific users without entering edit mode  
- [ ] See edit history / version count
- [ ] Quick-delete (with confirmation)
- [ ] Duplicate/fork item

**Non-owner viewing a public_edit item should see:**
- [x] Edit button (exists in item.html)
- [ ] Who last edited
- [ ] Number of contributors/edits
- [ ] "Fork to my account" if they want their own copy instead of editing in-place

**Non-owner viewing a public (view-only) item should see:**
- [ ] "Save/bookmark" button (covered by listhub-sxu)
- [ ] "Fork to my account" to create their own copy
- [ ] "Suggest edit" (like GitHub's fork-and-PR model — future)

### 5. Settings/permissions panel
- Could be a slide-out panel or modal instead of a full page reload
- Shows: visibility, sharing, edit history, API access info
- Accessible via gear icon on the item page

## Related Tickets
- listhub-60j: Consolidate edit experiences (shared infrastructure)
- listhub-sxu: Save/favorite lists (bookmark button)
- listhub-8wi: Per-user sharing with write permission
- listhub-z0t: Make lists publicly editable (public_edit visibility)
- listhub-2lg: Git history viewer (edit history)

## Priority
Start with inline visibility toggle for owners (highest impact, simplest change). Then add the fork/duplicate action. The full permissions panel is a bigger effort.

title: Add llms.txt and agent discovery standards
description: Add llms.txt, agents.json, .well-known/ai-plugin.json, OpenAPI spec to make ListHub discoverable by AI agents.

title: Search-or-create: fallback to new article when no results
description:
## Goal
When the user searches for something that doesn't exist yet, offer to create a new item with that title inline — no need to switch to "New item" flow.

This is the "search-or-create" pattern (Notion, Roam, Obsidian all do variants). It turns the search bar into a universal entry point: search to find, or search to create.

## Behavior

1. User types "research on karpathy" in the global search bar (Cmd+K)
2. Search returns 0 matches (or matches that aren't what the user wanted)
3. At the bottom of the results, a suggestion: **"Create new item: 'research on karpathy'"**
4. Click → create a new private item with that title (slug auto-generated) and open it in the editor
5. No intermediate forms, no confirmation dialogs, no folder pickers (put it at the root; user can move it later)

## Why this matters

- Lowest-friction "I had an idea, capture it now" path
- Works naturally with voice dictation / quick thought flows
- Parallels the agent-first pattern (just state intent, agent creates)
- Particularly useful for LLM wikis where items are cheap and numerous

## Open questions

**Should the created item default to private?** Yes — inherit the user's default visibility (usually private). User can change after.

**What folder does it go in?** Root by default. If the user was on a folder page when they searched, maybe offer "in {current folder}" as a second option.

**Should it be a separate button or just the last row in the results list?** Last row in results is lower-friction. Always visible, Enter to trigger.

**Agent integration**: should the API offer a search-or-create endpoint too? `POST /api/v1/search-or-create {q: "..."}` returns either the best match or a newly created item. Could be useful for agents but arguably just two calls (search + new) is fine.

## Related
- `listhub-95u` — Global search (this builds on that)
- `listhub-16c` — Agent search (agent analog)

## Sequencing
This is intentionally tagged P3 because it depends on the global search (listhub-95u) landing first. After that this is a small additive change: one extra row in the results list + one handler.

title: Autocomplete search of existing lists when creating new list
description:
## Goal
When creating a new list, provide autocomplete/semantic search of all existing list titles (both your own and public lists). Helps avoid duplicates and discover related content.

## UX
- Text input field at the top of the "New List" form (or integrated into the title field)
- As user types, show a dropdown of matching existing lists (fuzzy/FTS search)
- Results include: title, owner (@username), visibility badge
- Clicking a result opens it in a sidebar panel (split view) so you can reference it while creating
- Should search both your own lists AND all public lists
- Debounced search (200-300ms) to avoid hammering the API

## Implementation Options
1. **Client-side**: Fetch all public list titles on page load, filter in JS (fast but doesn't scale)
2. **API endpoint**: New `GET /api/v1/search/titles?q=...` that searches just titles (lightweight)
3. **Reuse existing search**: Use `GET /api/v1/search?q=...` but add a `titles_only` param

## Sidebar
- When you click a search result, open it in a right sidebar (similar to GitHub's file preview)
- Sidebar shows rendered content of the selected list
- Can close sidebar to go back to full-width editor
- Sidebar should be resizable or at least a reasonable split (e.g., 60/40)

## Related
- Could eventually become a general "reference panel" feature for any editing context

title: Multi-sheet CSV/spreadsheet support for list items
description:
## Context
Body Masters was imported from a Google Sheets export as a single markdown table (70 rows × 7 columns). The original sheet has **additional sheets/tabs** (referenced in the export filename: "Main list.csv" implies others exist). Only the Main list was exported.

## Problem
ListHub items are single markdown documents. There's no native concept of "a list with multiple sheets/tabs." For spreadsheets with related-but-distinct tables (Main list, Archive, Notes, Metadata, etc.), we force users to either:
1. Merge everything into one big doc (loses structure)
2. Create multiple separate items (loses the "this is one conceptual thing" relationship)

## Proposed solutions (pick one or combine)

### Option A: Folder-as-spreadsheet convention
A spreadsheet = a folder. Each sheet = an `index.md` section or a file within that folder. The folder itself is an item with `item_type: "spreadsheet"` and children are auto-rendered as tabs in the UI.

### Option B: Multi-section markdown
Single markdown doc with `## Sheet Name` headings. Each H2 section is treated as a sheet when importing/exporting to CSV. Cleaner file model but loses per-sheet metadata.

### Option C: First-class sheets in the data model
Add a `sheet` table joined to `item`. Each sheet has its own rows/columns stored as JSON or as a child markdown table. Most flexible, most work.

### Option D: Import tooling only
Don't add any new data model. Just ship a CLI that imports a multi-sheet CSV/XLSX and creates N linked items with cross-references in the frontmatter.

## Recommendation
Start with **Option D** (tooling only). Ship `listhub-import-sheets` that:
1. Reads a multi-sheet CSV/XLSX export
2. Creates one item per sheet with `file_path = spreadsheet-name/sheet-name.md`
3. Links them via a top-level `spreadsheet-name/index.md` that lists all sheets
4. Preserves original sheet order

This gives us multi-sheet support without schema changes. Upgrade to Option A or C later if usage justifies it.

## Related
- Body Masters import (current Main list only): `~/code/DataExports/body-masters.md`
- Google Sheets → CSV multi-sheet export: Takeout splits by tab automatically
- Obsidian's approach: each sheet is just a separate file, no special UI
- Notion's approach: database with views, first-class data model

## Open questions
- Should the markdown table be editable in-browser with a spreadsheet-ish UI (Handsontable, Luckysheet)? Big lift, but makes the "Google Sheets replacement" story real.
- XLSX support, or CSV only? CSV is trivial, XLSX needs a library.
- Formulas? (Probably no, ever. Trap.)

title: Create Python/JS client library for assistant integration
Keep API docs and GUI in sync for sharing/visibility
description: As visibility/sharing features are implemented, API docs AND web UI must stay in sync. No API-only or GUI-only features.

title: Consolidate edit experiences: directory, normal, and public edit
description:
## Problem
The directory edit experience and the normal item edit experience are completely different implementations with different UIs, creating inconsistency and maintenance burden.

### Current state — three separate edit experiences:

**1. Normal item edit (edit.html)**
- Milkdown rich-text WYSIWYG editor with raw toggle
- Title, slug, type, visibility, tags fields
- Markdown preview
- Full metadata controls

**2. Directory edit (directory_edit.html)**
- Plain textarea only (no rich editor)
- No title/metadata fields (just raw markdown)
- Commit message field (git-aware)
- Completely separate template and route

**3. Public edit (edit.html in public_edit_mode)**
- Reuses edit.html with some fields hidden (slug, type, visibility)
- Shows "you are editing as @user" banner
- Almost the same as normal edit but with restrictions

### What's inconsistent:
- Directory edit has NO rich editor (Milkdown) — just a plain textarea
- Directory edit has NO preview capability
- Directory edit doesn't show the file path in a useful way
- Normal edit doesn't have a commit message field (useful for version history)
- Three different code paths for what is conceptually "editing markdown content"

## Proposed Fix
Consolidate to a single edit experience that adapts to context:

1. **Use edit.html (with Milkdown) for ALL editing** — including directory pages
2. **Context-aware fields:**
   - Normal items: show title, slug, type, visibility, tags
   - Directory pages: show file path, commit message. Hide slug/type/visibility
   - Public edit: show the "editing as" banner. Hide slug/type/visibility
3. **Delete directory_edit.html** after migration
4. **Single route handler** that delegates to the right backend (DB vs git repo)

## Other consolidation items found:

### Visibility handling
- `public_edit` visibility is referenced inconsistently — some queries check `IN ('public', 'public_edit')` while others only check `'public'`
- landing.html only shows `visibility = 'public'` items (line 96 of views.py) — should also include `public_edit`
- The explore page (line 151) does include both

### Edit permission checks
- item.html checks `can_edit` variable, but profile.html checks `is_owner or visibility == 'public_edit'` separately
- Should be a single `can_edit` helper used everywhere

### Template duplication
- profile.html and dash.html both implement folder tree views with slightly different logic
- Tree rendering should be a shared partial/include

## Priority
Start with the directory edit → Milkdown migration (biggest UX gap), then consolidate the smaller items.

title: Bulk folder/vault import UI — drag a folder into the browser
description:
## Goal
A drag-drop folder upload UI that walks a local folder client-side and uploads each file to ListHub, preserving structure.

## Flow
1. User drags a folder (e.g. their Obsidian vault, a Drive folder export, a downloaded zip extraction) into a drop zone on /dash/upload
2. Client-side JS walks the folder via the Filesystem Access API or drag-drop file tree
3. For each file:
   - .md → use upload endpoint (listhub-ma7)
   - .docx/.doc/.rtf → convert via pandoc (listhub-dwa)
   - Image → upload as attachment (listhub-c6x)
   - Other → upload as binary attachment
4. Preserve folder hierarchy via file_path on each created item
5. Show a progress UI (X of Y files)
6. Optionally: bulk visibility setting (set all imported items to private/public/...)

## Why
This is the counterpart to the already-shipped server-side import script (`scripts/import_markdown.py`). That script is for power users with terminal access. This is for everyone else.

## Tech notes
- Browser Filesystem Access API is Chromium-only (no Safari yet) — fallback to drag-drop
- Need to handle large folders (100+ files) without locking up the browser — queue with throttled concurrency (5-10 at a time)
- Consider client-side ZIP import: drag a .zip, extract in browser via JSZip, process each file

## Related
- listhub-r62 — epic
- listhub-ma7, listhub-dwa, listhub-c6x — per-file-type upload
- listhub-74j — folder visibility (set for imported folders)
- `scripts/import_markdown.py` — existing server-side equivalent

title: Folder-level visibility with cascade (Drive-style folder permissions)
description:
## Vision
Folders in ListHub should be first-class entities with their own visibility, and items inside should inherit that visibility by default. Same model as Google Drive folder permissions.

## Current state
Folders are implicit — derived from item file_path at query time. No `folder` table, no per-folder metadata. Visibility is per-item only.

## Recommended approach: Option B (metadata files, git-native)
Rather than adding a `folder` table, store folder metadata in a `.folder.yaml` file at each folder level in the user git repo:

```yaml
# chronicpain/.folder.yaml
visibility: public
title: Chronic Pain Resources
description: Bay Area practitioners I have worked with
```

The tree builder reads this overlay on top of the existing file_path derivation. Items with no explicit visibility inherit from the nearest ancestor folder. Items with explicit visibility override.

### Why Option B
- **Zero schema migration** — existing items keep working
- **Git-native** — permissions survive clone/push/pull automatically
- **Agent-friendly** — agents can write `.folder.yaml` like any other markdown file
- **Upgrade-compatible** — if we ever want query caching, add a folder table that indexes these files

### Alternative options rejected
- **Option A: first-class folder table** — cleanest data model but biggest upfront cost; backfill from existing file_paths; needs git sync adapter for `.folder.yaml` anyway
- **Option C: extend item_tag with folder properties** — hacky, no proper inheritance

## Design decisions to make
- [ ] **Cascade mode**: soft inherit (item explicit visibility wins) vs hard cascade (folder overwrites items on change). I recommend SOFT.
- [ ] **Bulk change action**: a separate "apply to all items in folder" action that DOES hard-cascade on user request
- [ ] **Default for new items** in a folder: inherit from folder (so new items in a public folder default to public)
- [ ] **Conflict resolution**: explicit item visibility always wins over folder default
- [ ] **Empty folders**: can still have a visibility to serve as default for future children
- [ ] **Metadata files themselves**: treat `.folder.yaml` like `.gitignore` — not listed, not rendered, always loaded server-side
- [ ] **Migration path**: for existing folders that have implicit visibility aggregated from children, optionally auto-create `.folder.yaml` with the inferred visibility on first edit

## Implementation sketch

### 1. Tree builder overlay (30 lines)
```python
def load_folder_metadata(owner_username, folder_path):
    # Read /git/<user>.git/<folder_path>/.folder.yaml via _git_read
    raw = _git_read(user_repo, "show", f"HEAD:{folder_path}/.folder.yaml")
    return yaml.safe_load(raw) if raw else None
```

### 2. API endpoints
- `GET /api/v1/folders/:path` → return metadata
- `PUT /api/v1/folders/:path` → set metadata (creates the `.folder.yaml` file via a commit on the users bare repo)
- `PUT /api/v1/folders/:path/visibility` → shortcut for the common case
- `POST /api/v1/folders/:path/apply-visibility` → bulk cascade to all items in the folder

### 3. Effective visibility resolver
When rendering an item or deciding access, resolve its effective visibility by walking up ancestors until one has an explicit setting. Cache per-request.

### 4. UI
- Right-click / hover action on folder rows: "Set folder visibility"
- Folder header on `/@user/path/` zoomed folder page: visibility badge + quick toggle
- Sidebar folder rows: visibility icon already renders via the aggregation logic from earlier, but now reads from metadata instead of aggregating

## Vision alignment
This is the key unlock for "agent-native Google Drive replacement." Once folders have permissions, ListHub matches Drive on the most important permission primitive, while keeping the markdown-first advantage.

## Related
- listhub-r62 — Agent-native Google Drive replacement (epic this supports)
- listhub-ftr — Central home doc (home item lives at root, this pattern lets the root be "private folder with one public home item")
- listhub-f18 — Public/private home page alternatives (the <!-- private --> block is the line-level complement)
- listhub-2zv — File bucket architecture
- listhub-c6x — Inline image support

## No real blockers
This is purely design work plus a few hundred lines of code. The hardest part is agreeing on cascade semantics, not the implementation.

comments.author: Jacob Cole
comments.text:
## Codex second opinion: cascade is a foot-gun

Codex pushed back on my soft-cascade recommendation: visibility inheritance should be OPT-IN, not default.

> If a binary has no sidecar, it should not be public even if the folder is public. That prevents accidental exposure of newly uploaded files.

### Revised cascade rules

- Default visibility is `private` unless explicitly set on the item
- Folder cascade only applies if `.folder.yaml` has `inherit_visibility: true` (or `cascade: true`)
- No implicit inheritance — a file with no sidecar/frontmatter visibility defaults to `private`, regardless of folder settings
- Explicit override — a file can always set its own visibility to override the folder default

### Rationale
More conservative than Google Drive default but safer for the "one wrong file leaks everything" scenario. Users who want Drive-style inheritance explicitly opt in per folder.

See listhub-r62.1 for full Codex critique.

comments.text:

The **description above** is my **original pre-Codex proposal** (written 2026-04-07, before asking Codex for a second opinion).

comments.author: Jacob Cole
comments.text:
## 🎯 RESOLUTION: cascade-by-default with visible confirmation (user decision 2026-04-07)

After reading the Codex critique, Jacob's position is that **cascade-by-default is the correct UX** (matches Drive expectations), and Codex's safety concern is better addressed via **visible upload-time confirmation** than via a worse default.

### Final cascade semantics (revised)

1. **Default behavior = cascade** (matches Google Drive)
   - A file uploaded into a `public` folder → becomes `public`
   - A file uploaded into a `private` folder → becomes `private`
   - No `inherit_visibility: true` opt-in required on the folder
   - This applies at **item creation time**, not retroactively

2. **Items can still override** their inherited visibility explicitly
   - Frontmatter (markdown) or sidecar (binary) with explicit `visibility:` wins
   - If unset, item resolves to parent folder's visibility

3. **Changing folder visibility does NOT retroactively rewrite children** (this is where Codex IS right)
   - Editing `.folder.yaml` from `private` to `public` affects only NEW items going forward
   - To apply the new setting to existing children, user must invoke an **explicit "apply to all items in this folder"** bulk action
   - This prevents the "I edited one setting and leaked 100 files" scenario

4. **Upload-time visibility must be visible** (the safety concern, solved at the UX layer)
   - Every upload flow (web drag-drop, API POST, git push) must surface the **resolved visibility** before commit
   - Web UI: "This file will be 🌐 PUBLIC (inherited from folder). [Keep public] [Make private instead]"
   - API: response body includes `"resolved_visibility": "public"` and `"visibility_source": "folder_inherited"` so agents can see what will happen
   - Git push: post-receive hook logs resolved visibility for each new file; future tooling could emit a pre-push warning

5. **UI affordance: inherited vs explicit**
   - Items with explicit visibility show a solid badge: `🌐 public`
   - Items with inherited visibility show a hollow / subscripted badge: `🌐 public (from folder)` or `↳ 🌐`
   - Makes "which items will flip if I change the folder setting" visually obvious

### Why this over Codex's "opt-in cascade" proposal

- **Drive has 2 billion users** operating on cascade-by-default. It's the expected model. Breaking it without a strong reason loses new users immediately.
- **Codex's concern is real but UX-solvable.** The fear is "I upload to a public folder and don't realize it went public." The fix is showing the user what will happen BEFORE it happens, not making the default wrong.
- **Folders are the permission surface.** If cascade is opt-in, folder permissions become decorative — every file still needs per-item config. That defeats the purpose of having folder-level permissions at all.
- **Retroactive rewrites are the actual foot-gun** — and both Jacob and Codex agree we should NOT do retroactive cascade on folder edits. That's the real danger. We address it explicitly via the "apply to all items" bulk action model.

### Implementation implications

- The `.folder.yaml` file needs a `visibility:` field that serves as the **default for new items in this folder**
- Item creation (via API, web form, or git push) resolves visibility by walking ancestor folders until it finds an explicit setting
- Item update preserves the resolved visibility at the time of creation (stored as explicit) unless the user uses the bulk "re-inherit" action
- Sidecar files for binaries follow the same rules as markdown frontmatter

### Status
This supersedes my original "soft cascade" wording AND supersedes Codex's "opt-in only" pushback. Specific rule: **cascade-by-default at creation, never retroactive on folder edits**.

Ticket description is still the pre-Codex draft (now superseded by this resolution comment + the Codex comment). When implementing, follow these resolved semantics, not the description.

title: Rethink lists vs categories/folders naming and UI
description:
## Problem
The homepage says "Featured Lists" but those entries are really folders/categories from the community directory (tech, cooking, reading, etc.), not individual lists. This is confusing — a "list" implies a flat collection of items, while these are hierarchical categories containing multiple lists.

## Areas to Address

### 1. Homepage "Featured Lists" section
- These are community directory categories, not lists
- Consider renaming to "Featured Topics", "Categories", "Collections", or "Directories"
- Or rethink what goes there — maybe show actual popular lists alongside directory categories

### 2. Community directory folder icons
- Directory tree currently has plain text with triangle toggles
- Add folder icons (📁/📂) to make the hierarchy more visually obvious
- File icons (📄) for individual .md entries
- This helps distinguish folders from leaf items at a glance

### 3. Broader naming taxonomy
- "List" = a single user-created document (note, list, document)
- "Category" / "Folder" / "Topic" = a directory node in the community tree
- "Collection" = could mean a curated set of lists from multiple users
- Need consistent terminology across the site, API docs, and UI

### 4. Consider whether lists and categories are the same thing
- Right now they're separate systems: lists are DB items, categories are git folders
- Should a user be able to create a "category" that contains other people's lists?
- This touches on the broader question of whether ListHub is a wiki, a directory, or both

## Quick wins for tomorrow
- Rename "Featured Lists" on homepage
- Add folder/file icons to community directory tree
- Review all user-facing copy for list vs category confusion

title: Agent onboarding UX: one-click personal agent flow + standards research
description:
## Question
How do we make the "fully agent-friendly app" workflow even more obvious and frictionless? Is there a standard or emerging convention for this?

## Vision
A user visiting ListHub should see something like an "Open in your agent" button — they click it, and their personal AI agent (Claude, ChatGPT, Codex, etc.) is launched with the right context to register, get an API key, and start working with ListHub immediately.

## Brainstorm Areas

### 1. "Open in Agent" UX pattern
- Button on landing page / item pages: "Open in Claude" / "Open in ChatGPT" / "Open in your agent"
- What does this actually do? Options:
  - Copy a prompt to clipboard that the user pastes into their agent
  - Deep link into a specific agent (claude:// protocol? ChatGPT custom GPT link?)
  - Generate a pre-filled prompt URL (like ChatGPT share links)
  - Show a modal with "give these instructions to your agent" + copyable text
- The prompt would include: base URL, registration curl, what the agent can do

### 2. In-App Agent Sidebar
- ListHub itself could have an embedded "Your Agent" sidebar panel
- User connects their Claude/OpenAI API key in settings
- The sidebar IS their personal agent, with ListHub tools pre-loaded
- Think: Notion AI sidebar, but powered by the user's own agent
- Could start simple: text input → sends to Claude API with ListHub MCP tools
- Items on the current page become context for the agent automatically

### 3. Cortex as the Personal Agent Layer
- **Cortex** (github.com/IdeaFlowCo/cortex) is already a cloud-based Claude CLI platform
- Each user gets an isolated Linux workspace with Claude CLI pre-installed
- Has: real-time chat, file browser, web terminal, session continuity
- **The connection**: ListHub items could be the user's knowledge base inside Cortex
  - Cortex workspace auto-clones user's ListHub repo
  - Claude in Cortex gets ListHub MCP tools pre-configured
  - User's lists/notes/docs are always available as context
- **"Open in Cortex"** button on ListHub: launches your personal Claude agent with this list/item as context
- Cortex already has auth, provisioning, WebSocket chat — just needs ListHub integration
- CloudCLI (cortex/cloudcli) is a local-first variant that could also integrate

### 4. Existing standards to research
- **llms.txt** (already implemented) — machine-readable site description
- **agents.json** / **.well-known/ai-plugin.json** — OpenAI's ChatGPT plugin spec (deprecated but pattern exists)
- **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** — Anthropic's standard for tool servers. ListHub as MCP server = any Claude session gets native access
- **OpenAPI spec** — standard API description, agents can auto-generate tools from it
- **Tool Use schemas** — include recommended tool definitions in llms.txt or a dedicated endpoint
- **A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol** — Google's emerging standard
- **Agent Cards** — part of A2A, machine-readable agent capability descriptions

### 5. "Fully agent-friendly app" checklist
What makes an app truly agent-friendly? Draft a checklist:
- [x] Programmatic registration (no browser needed)
- [x] API key in one call
- [x] llms.txt
- [ ] OpenAPI spec (machine-parseable endpoint definitions)
- [ ] MCP server (agents can use ListHub as a tool natively)
- [ ] "Open in agent" button on the site
- [ ] In-app agent sidebar
- [ ] Agent-optimized error messages (structured JSON, actionable)
- [ ] Rate limit headers (so agents can self-throttle)
- [ ] Webhook/event support (so agents can react to changes)
- [ ] Cortex integration (personal agent with ListHub pre-loaded)

### 6. Could this become a standard itself?
ListHub could define and promote an "Agent-Friendly App" badge/standard:
- A spec that says "if your app has these N things, it's agent-friendly"
- A verification endpoint (like robots.txt but for agents)
- Could list agent-friendly apps in the ListHub directory itself
- The agent-friendly-apps list at /@jacobreal/agent-friendly-apps becomes the registry

## Open Questions
- Which agent platforms support deep linking / launch URLs?
- Is there a de facto "open in agent" protocol emerging?
- Should we build an MCP server for ListHub? (connects to listhub-3q6)
- What does the "Open in your agent" button actually look like in the UI?
- How deep should the Cortex ↔ ListHub integration go? Shared auth? Shared data layer?
- Should the in-app sidebar be its own feature or just link out to Cortex?

title: Pioneer the agent-first web: persistent agent bar, Chrome extension, standard
description:
## Vision
Pioneer the "agent-first web" — a new design paradigm where websites are built from the ground up for AI agents to use, with the human UI as a complementary layer. ListHub can be the first site to fully embody this.

## Core Concepts

### 1. Persistent Agent Bar
- A text input bar at the top of every page (like a search bar, but for agent commands)
- Connected to your personal Claude/Cortex agent via API
- Context-aware: knows what page you're on and suggests relevant actions
- "Add this to my list" / "Save this for later" / "Share with Alice"
- Replaces traditional UI flows with natural language

### 2. Agent as Browser Extension
- Chrome extension that provides a personal agent sidebar on ANY website
- Not per-app AI — YOUR agent that follows you across the web
- On agent-friendly sites (like ListHub), it auto-discovers APIs via llms.txt
- On regular sites, it can read the page content and take notes
- Connects to Cortex for persistent memory and session continuity

### 3. Context-Aware Actions
- Agent sees what page you're viewing and suggests relevant actions
- Viewing a list? "Would you like to fork this to your account?"
- Viewing someone's profile? "Save their best lists to your collection?"
- The page provides structured metadata that the agent can parse

### 4. Agent-First Design Principles (Manifesto)
- Every page is an API: if a human can do it, an agent can do it
- Self-service onboarding: no OAuth flows, no approval queues, one POST
- Your agent, everywhere: not per-app AI, your personal agent
- Context-aware: the agent knows where you are and what's relevant

### 5. Standard for Agent-Friendly Websites
- Could ListHub define a standard other sites adopt?
- llms.txt (discovery) + registration API + structured actions
- "Agent-Friendly" badge/certification
- Chrome extension could work with any site that follows the standard

## Implementation Phases

**Phase 1: Agent Bar on ListHub**
- Add the persistent input bar to the ListHub layout
- Wire it to the ListHub API (register, create, append, search)
- Start with client-side Claude API call (user provides their own key)

**Phase 2: Cortex Integration**
- Connect the agent bar to Cortex backend
- Shared session continuity — agent remembers previous conversations
- No API key needed — Cortex handles auth

**Phase 3: Chrome Extension**
- Build extension that adds the agent sidebar to any website
- Auto-detect agent-friendly sites via llms.txt
- Context extraction from page content
- Cortex as the backend for all agent actions

**Phase 4: Standard & Ecosystem**
- Document the agent-first web standard
- Build a registry of agent-friendly sites (on ListHub itself!)
- Provide badges/verification
- SDKs for other sites to become agent-friendly

## Related Work
- Mockup: /mockups/homepage-agentfirst.html
- listhub-78c: Agent onboarding UX brainstorm (related but narrower scope)
- listhub-3q6: llms.txt and agent discovery standards
- Cortex project: github.com/IdeaFlowCo/cortex

## Open Questions
- Build on top of MCP? Or independent protocol?
- Chrome extension vs native app vs both?
- Revenue model: free standard, paid Cortex hosting?
- How to handle sites that don't have llms.txt yet? (scraping? prompting?)

title: Google Drive connector: one-click import of a Drive folder into ListHub
description:
## Goal
One-click import of a Google Drive folder into ListHub, converting each Google Doc to markdown.

## Flow
1. User connects their Google account via OAuth (scope: drive.readonly + docs.readonly)
2. User picks a Drive folder from a picker UI
3. ListHub walks the folder recursively
4. For each Google Doc: export as .docx, convert to markdown via pandoc (listhub-dwa)
5. For each .md / .txt file: upload as-is
6. For each image: upload as attachment (listhub-c6x)
7. For each Sheet: export as CSV, create a markdown table item (listhub-4jm — multi-sheet CSV support)
8. Preserve folder hierarchy as ListHub folders (listhub-74j — folder visibility)
9. Default visibility: private

## Why this matters
This is THE differentiator for the "agent-native Drive replacement" pitch. Users can come from Drive, one-click their existing knowledge base over, and immediately get:
- Public URLs for anything they want to share
- Git access to everything
- Agent-editable markdown with YAML frontmatter
- Per-item and per-folder visibility the agent can set at write time

## Tech notes
- Google Drive API v3 + Google Docs API
- Need to handle: permissions (folders OAuth cant see), large files, rate limits, .gdoc shortcuts
- Conversion: Google Doc → .docx → markdown (via pandoc). Alternative: Google Doc → HTML → markdown via html2text or similar
- Incremental import: track drive_file_id on each imported item so future syncs only touch changed files

## Scope for v1
- One-time import (not continuous sync)
- Docs + markdown/text files only
- No sheets, no images (add in future passes)

## Related
- listhub-r62 — epic
- listhub-dwa — docx converter (dependency)
- listhub-ma7 — markdown file upload (dependency)
- listhub-c6x — image support (for future doc-with-images)
- listhub-4jm — multi-sheet CSV (for future Sheets import)
- listhub-74j — folder visibility (to preserve Drive folder permissions)

## Prior art
- Notion has a Google Drive importer (not great, lossy)
- Obsidian has community plugins for Drive sync (niche, unmaintained)
- Nobody has built an AGENT-NATIVE Drive importer that preserves the per-item visibility model ListHub has. This is a green field.

title: Global search + tag filter + sidebar search
description:
## Goal
A first-class search and filter experience across ListHub that spans your own items, the community directory, and (eventually) other users' public content. The key primitive: a global search bar, with scoped fallbacks.

## Three related features, one ticket (for V1 scoping)

### 1. Global search bar
- Always accessible from the top header (Cmd+K / Ctrl+K keyboard shortcut, per convention from Linear, Notion, GitHub)
- Queries across all visible content: your own items (all visibilities) + public items from everyone + community directory
- Returns grouped results: "Your docs", "Community", "Public (others)"
- Each result = title, path, snippet, visibility badge, click to open
- Server-side: FTS5 with `visibility IN ('public', 'public_edit') OR owner_id = :me` filtering

### 2. Tag filter
- Browse and filter items by tag, surfaced both in the main content area (for list pages) and possibly as a sidebar section or pill row
- Tag autocomplete in the search bar: typing `#` should suggest tags
- Per-page "filter by tag" controls on dashboard, profile, explore, directory
- Tag cloud or tag index page showing all tags and item counts

### 3. Sidebar search / filter
- A small filter input at the top of each sidebar section that fuzzy-filters that section's tree
- Instant feedback (JS-side, no round trip)
- Clearing resets the tree to full
- Independent from the global search; for when you already know you're looking within "Your docs" or "Community"

## Design questions

**Should sidebar search be a filter over the loaded tree (client-side fuzzy) or a live query (server-side)?**
Client-side is simpler for V1 and fast for trees of <500 items. Server-side scales better. Start client-side.

**Should global search be client-side at all, or always server?**
Always server. FTS5 is already wired up via `GET /api/v1/search`, just needs a UI and visibility-scoping fix (currently scoped to owner only, needs a public+owner variant).

**What results look like when there's no match?**
See listhub-3wv (search-or-create fallback) — that's where this pattern extends.

## Scope for V1
1. Global search bar in header (Cmd+K modal)
2. Server endpoint: `GET /api/v1/search/global?q=...&scope=mine|public|all`
3. Sidebar per-section client-side filter input
4. Basic tag filter on the dashboard and explore pages

Defer to V2:
- Tag autocomplete in search
- Tag cloud / index page
- Semantic search (embeddings)
- Cross-user private results (shared-with-me)

## Related
- `listhub-3wv` — Search-or-create fallback (the "ask/create" flow)
- `listhub-16c` — Agent-friendly search (API-side considerations)
- Existing `GET /api/v1/search` — already has keyword search but owner-scoped only

title: Download user git repo as ZIP — full for owner, filtered for others
description:
## Goal
Add a "Download as ZIP" button on user profile pages and the dashboard that bundles up the user's git repo as a downloadable archive.

## Behavior

### When the viewer IS the owner
- Downloads the **full** bare git repo (or a snapshot of all items as markdown files)
- Includes private + shared + public + public_edit
- Preserves the folder structure (file_path)
- Includes YAML frontmatter on each file (title, slug, visibility, type, tags)
- Includes `.folder.yaml` files when listhub-74j ships

### When the viewer is NOT the owner
- Downloads a **filtered** view: only items with visibility in (`public`, `public_edit`, `unlisted`)
- Same structure preservation
- Could be served live (no caching) since the filter is dynamic per requestor

## Implementation sketch

1. **Server-side ZIP generation**: walk the user's items, write each as a markdown file with frontmatter to an in-memory ZIP, return as `application/zip`
2. **Endpoint**: `GET /api/v1/users/:username/download.zip` (no auth = filtered, with auth as owner = full)
3. **UI**: "Download as ZIP" button on `/@username` profile page next to the Home button. Possibly also a "Download my repo" button on `/dash`.
4. **Filename**: `{username}-listhub-{date}.zip`
5. **Filter logic**: same visibility check used elsewhere (`visibility IN ('public', 'public_edit', 'unlisted')`)

## Why
- Lets users grab their own data offline (data portability is a core ListHub principle)
- Lets non-owners take a snapshot of someone's public content for their own archive (like cloning a public GitHub repo)
- Complements the existing `git clone` access via the bare repo HTTP endpoint, which is more developer-shaped
- ZIP is the universal "save this for me" format — works for non-developers

## Related
- listhub-ev1 — Clone URL and download button (parent feature, still open)
- listhub-xtc — GitHub-like repo UX epic
- listhub-74j — Folder-level visibility (the filter has to walk this tree once it ships)
- listhub-r62 — Agent-native Drive replacement epic
- The git_sync.py module already generates per-item markdown with frontmatter — reuse that logic for the ZIP build

## Edge cases
- Very large repos (100MB+): stream the response, don't build in memory. Use Python's `zipfile` with a streaming wrapper.
- Empty repos: return a zip with just a README explaining the repo is empty
- Private + non-existent users: 404
- Concurrent downloads: cache the filtered ZIP for ~5 minutes by (user, viewer_visibility_set) hash to avoid rebuilding

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `GET /@username/download.zip` returns a ZIP with filtered or full content
- [ ] Owner sees "Download my full repo" button on /@username and /dash
- [ ] Non-owner sees "Download public items" button on /@username
- [ ] ZIP file uses canonical YAML frontmatter format
- [ ] Folder structure is preserved
- [ ] Tested with @jacobreal (90+ items, mixed visibility)

title: Directory/showcase view template (like renaissance.engineer, deep-dialogues)
description:
Create a view template/theme system so ListHub items and collections can be displayed in rich, curated layouts — beyond the current simple markdown rendering.

Inspiration:
- renaissance.engineer: people directory with expandable profile cards, discipline tags/badges, vision statements, project links, stats
- deep-dialogues.ideaflow.app: conversation/dialogue display with speaker labels, threaded responses

Both are essentially curated lists with rich rendering.

Design options:
1. Template system: items can specify a display template (directory, showcase, dialogue, cards)
2. Custom CSS per item/collection
3. Structured data in frontmatter that templates consume

Key insight: ListHub already has the data layer (items, tags, markdown). What's missing is the presentation layer — templates that turn a list of items into a visually compelling page.

Template types to support:
- Directory (cards with metadata, filtering, search)
- Showcase (featured items with rich media)
- Dialogue (conversation format with speaker labels)
- Cards (grid layout with thumbnails)

Related: listhub-ogp (directory UI specifically), listhub-00k (flowtools as first directory content)

Inline image support for markdown items (Obsidian-style ![[image.png]])
description: ## Goal Support inline images in markdown items, at minimum matching what Obsidian does. ## Current state ListHub items are markdown-only. Images can be referenced via URL (`![](https://...)`) but: - No upload pipeline - No inline image storage alongside items - No Obsidian-style wikilink image embeds (`![[screenshot.png]]`) ## Proposed scope — v1 (Obsidian parity) ### Upload + storage - `POST /api/v1/files` (multipart) → returns `{url, hash, size, mime_type}` - Store binary content-addressed (sha256) in a blob store - Initial blob store: local disk under `LISTHUB_BLOB_ROOT` (simple, migrate later) - Files are owned — attached to a user via `file_owner` or similar - Max upload size: 25 MB initially - Allowed types: image/png, image/jpeg, image/webp, image/gif, image/svg+xml (no executable SVG) ### Markdown rendering - Support standard markdown `![alt](url)` — already works for remote URLs - Support local refs `![alt](/files/.png)` - Support Obsidian-style `![[screenshot.png]]` that resolves to the owner attachment - Render as `` with lazy loading, cap width with CSS ### Visibility inheritance - Files inherit the visibility of the item that references them - If an item is public, its images are fetchable publicly - If an item is private, the image URL requires auth (signed URL or session check) ### Editor UX (v1) - Drag/drop onto the Milkdown editor → upload → insert markdown ref - Paste image from clipboard → same flow ## Obsidian parity checklist - [x] ![alt](url) syntax - [ ] ![[image.png]] wikilink syntax - [ ] Drag-drop upload in editor - [ ] Clipboard paste upload - [ ] Image preview in edit mode - [ ] Image resizing via CSS / markdown width attr ## Nice-to-haves (future) - Image alt text inference via vision model on upload - Thumbnail generation for image grids - EXIF stripping for privacy - Content-addressed deduplication (same image referenced from multiple items shares one blob) ## Related - listhub-2zv — file bucket / upload architecture (foundational decision for THIS ticket) - listhub-4jm — multi-sheet CSV support (adjacent non-markdown format) - listhub-2zb — auto-push to GitHub (images need to be pushed too)

title: Add graph database support for item relationships/links
Audit sidebar tree expand/collapse design and behavior
description:
## Audit findings (2026-04-07)

Two tree components exist and have DIFFERENT behaviors:

### `templates/tree.html` + `static/tree.js` (used by dash.html, profile.html)
- **Toggle**: entire folder row is clickable to toggle collapse. `tree.js`
  `initTree()` attaches a click listener to `.tree-row--folder`.
- **Nested collapse awareness**: expand walks descendants and respects the
  collapsed state of subfolders (smart — good).
- **Link protection**: `if (e.target.closest(a)) return;` prevents toggle
  when clicking a link inside the row.
- **Chevron**: was decorative only (rotated via CSS on `.collapsed` class).
  **Partially fixed in commit cc768f3**: chevron now has explicit click
  handler with `stopPropagation + preventDefault` and `cursor: pointer`.

### `templates/_sidebar_tree.html` (used by _sidebar.html)
- **Toggle**: explicit chevron button with `aria-expanded` attribute.
- **Folder link**: folder name is a separate `<a class="sb-folder-link">`
  that navigates to the folder view.
- **Different from tree.html**: this one already had the right separation
  of concerns.

## Remaining issues

- [ ] **No persistent state**: refresh loses collapse state. Neither tree
  persists via localStorage. Should persist keyed on `(owner, view)`.
- [ ] **No default-expand behavior**: all folders start expanded. Nice to
  have: auto-expand the folder containing the current item when viewing
  an item page from the sidebar.
- [ ] **Empty folder placeholder rendering**: tree.html shows `<div
  class="tree-row--empty">` with "Empty" text for folders with 0 files.
  Good feature but worth checking if it looks right with the new folder
  drill-down link.
- [ ] **Two separate tree implementations**: `tree.html` vs
  `_sidebar_tree.html`. Consider consolidating. They have inconsistent
  class names (`tree-*` vs `sb-*`), styling, and behavior. File a followup
  ticket for consolidation.
- [ ] **Keyboard navigation**: neither tree supports arrow-key nav.
  Should file followup ticket.
- [ ] **aria-expanded**: only _sidebar_tree.html sets aria-expanded. tree.html
  does not, so it is less screen-reader friendly.

## Status
Partial fix landed (kdg + chevron click) in cc768f3. The persistent state,
default-expand, and tree consolidation are follow-up work. This ticket stays
open with a checklist above; spawn child tickets as you decide to tackle each.

title: Document converter: .docx / .doc / .rtf → markdown on import
description:
## Goal
Convert uploaded .docx, .doc, and .rtf files to markdown at import time, creating a ListHub item from each.

## Approach
- Use pandoc via subprocess for the conversion: `pandoc -f docx -t gfm -o out.md input.docx`
- Preserve headings, lists, tables, bold/italic, links
- Images get uploaded as attachments (requires listhub-c6x + listhub-2zv)
- First pass can skip images; inline image support comes later

## Scope
- **Trigger**: user drops a .docx file into the upload zone
- **Server-side**: pandoc converts to markdown, the result becomes an item
- **Frontmatter**: title derived from first heading or filename, visibility private, type document
- **Error handling**: if pandoc fails, return the error to the user; keep the uploaded file in a temp location for retry

## Tech notes
- pandoc is already a Ubuntu apt package — `apt install pandoc` on prod
- Alternative: python-docx library for .docx only, but pandoc handles more formats in one tool
- Conversion quality for complex docs (nested tables, styled content) will be imperfect — thats fine, markdown is lossier than docx by design

## Related
- listhub-r62 — epic
- listhub-ma7 — markdown upload (prerequisite for the upload pipeline)
- listhub-c6x — image support needed for image-containing docs

title: Add unlisted visibility option

comments.author: Jacob Cole
comments.text:
Implementation pushed to feature/unlisted-visibility on origin (https://github.com/tmad4000/listhub/pull/new/feature/unlisted-visibility).

== Files changed ==
- api.py: VALID_VISIBILITIES gets unlisted; /api/v1/public/<user>/<slug> allows unlisted reads
- views.py: public_item() and short_link() allow unlisted (note: this change landed in main as part of commit 5c04957 by another agents bd add . — see ORCHESTRATION NOTE below)
- static/style.css: .badge-unlisted (orange tone)
- templates/edit.html: <option value=unlisted>Unlisted (anyone with link)
- templates/dash.html: Unlisted filter tab + see-no-evil-monkey badge
- templates/profile.html: see-no-evil-monkey badge for owner-only view
- templates/item.html: unlisted chip on item header

== Tests run (against fresh local Flask instance on Mac mini) ==
PASS: POST /api/v1/items/new with visibility=unlisted accepted
PASS: GET /@testuser/my-unlisted-list returns HTTP 200 (HTML)
PASS: GET /api/v1/public/testuser/my-unlisted-list returns HTTP 200 with visibility=unlisted
PASS: GET /@testuser/my-private-list returns HTTP 404 (unauth control)
PASS: GET /@testuser/my-public-list returns HTTP 200 (control)
PASS: /@testuser (anon profile) shows ONLY my-public-list (no unlisted, no private)
PASS: /browse shows ONLY my-public-list (no unlisted)
PASS: /community shows none (no public_edit items)
PASS: / (landing) shows ONLY my-public-list
PASS: /browse?q=stuff (FTS search) excludes unlisted
PASS: /dash?visibility=unlisted&view=list (authed owner) shows ONLY my-unlisted-list (1 item-card)
PASS: /dash?visibility=public&view=list shows ONLY my-public-list
PASS: /dash?view=list shows all 3 (no filter)
PASS: filter-tab Unlisted is rendered and active when selected
PASS: edit form shows unlisted option, selected for unlisted item
PASS: item view page shows unlisted badge

== Architecture ==
Discovery queries (landing, community, browse, profile non-owner, focused-user sidebar) all already use visibility IN (public, public_edit), which naturally excludes unlisted — no query changes were needed. The owner s own dashboard/sidebar see ALL items including unlisted (correct).

No schema migration: visibility column is TEXT with no CHECK constraint.

== ORCHESTRATION NOTE — coordination conflict ==
While I was making the feature edits, another agent (working on listhub-lav sidebar V1.1) was concurrently committing on the same checkout. They ran git add . which swept up my views.py edits into their sidebar V1.1 commit. End result: main now contains commit 5c04957 (sidebar V1.1) which actually includes BOTH the sidebar work AND my views.py unlisted access checks (with a misleading commit message). I split out the sidebar-only changes onto branch sidebar-v1.1-preserved (4 files, no views.py) for reference.

The unlisted feature is FUNCTIONALLY COMPLETE on feature/unlisted-visibility branch — when merged to main, all 7 file changes are present (since views.py is already in main from the bundled commit).

== What is left for the parent ==
1. Review feature/unlisted-visibility (1 unlisted commit + 1 bd-sync commit)
2. Decide whether to:
   a) Merge as-is (clean unlisted feature, with the views.py change already in main)
   b) Optionally rewrite the misleading 5c04957 commit message (would require a force-push to main, NOT recommended)
3. Deploy to Lightsail when ready (I did NOT push to lightsail per task instructions)
4. Optionally delete sidebar-v1.1-preserved branch (it is just a diagnostic of what the cleanly-split sidebar commit would have looked like)

== Followup tickets to consider ==
None — feature is self-contained. The discovery query patterns naturally support adding more visibility levels in the future.

title: Clone URL and download button for repos/lists
description:
## Goal
Add a prominent clone URL and download button to user profile/repo pages, giving the GitHub "green Code button" feel.

## Features
1. **Clone URL display**: Show a copyable `git clone listhub.globalbr.ai/@username.git` URL on each user's public page
2. **Download as ZIP**: Button to download a snapshot of a user's public items as a .zip or .tar.gz
3. **Generated public repo**: Even if the actual bare repo contains private items, generate a filtered clone-friendly version with only public content
4. **Community directory**: The community repo should be especially easy to clone — featured on the explore page

## Implementation Notes
- ListHub already has bare git repos per user — the clone URL could serve these directly via git-http-backend or smart HTTP protocol
- For privacy: need a git filter that strips private items before serving (or maintain a separate public-only bare repo that's updated on each commit)
- Download ZIP is simpler: just archive the public items as markdown files in a folder structure
- Consider showing the clone command in a modal/dropdown like GitHub does

## UX Reference
GitHub's "Code" button shows:
- HTTPS clone URL (copyable)
- SSH clone URL
- GitHub CLI command
- "Download ZIP" link
- "Open with GitHub Desktop"

We don't need all of these — start with HTTPS clone URL + Download ZIP.

title: Public/private split for user home page (and per-line visibility)
description:
## Goal
Consider alternatives to the current approach for mixing public and private content in the user home page.

## Shipped implementation (2026-04-07)

**Option A + Option C** (combined):
1. **Option A: Two separate pages** — `/@username/home` is public, `/dash` is private dashboard owner-only. Cross-linked via Home buttons.
2. **Option C: HTML-comment private blocks** — any item can contain `<!-- private -->...<!-- /private -->` blocks that are stripped at render time for non-owners. Works across ALL items, not just home. Documented in `/llms.txt` and `/api/docs`. Implemented in `views.py:render_md(is_owner=)` and `strip_private_blocks()`. Also filters the copy-to-clipboard textarea.

See commit: 771e359 and 92920aa.

## Options NOT yet shipped (for future consideration)

### Option B: Per-line visibility markers (markdown extension)
Instead of block-level comments, support per-line markers like:
```
- Public item
- [private] Secret item only I see
- Another public item
```
Pros: finer granularity, more natural.
Cons: needs a real markdown extension, editor UX, and an agent contract.

### Option D: Encrypted per-section (age or libsodium sealed boxes)
Server cannot see the content at all. Owner decrypts client-side.
Pros: truly private — content is protected even from DB access.
Cons: key management complexity, agent-hostile (agents cant read what they cant decrypt), requires frontend crypto.

### Option E: content + content_private fields on the item schema
Separate database columns for public vs private. Cleaner than inline markers, ugly for narrative flow.

### Option F: Dashboard composes home
`/dash` renders the home page content at the top + additional private sections below. Single source for home, plus extras.

## Decision criteria for revisit

Consider revisiting if:
- Users find `<!-- private -->` too clunky for frequent use
- A common pattern emerges where per-line privacy would be better than per-block
- Encryption becomes necessary for compliance or trust reasons
- The copy-to-clipboard / API-read surface keeps leaking private content in unexpected ways

## Related
- listhub-ftr — home page feature (done)
- Commits: 92920aa (feat: private content blocks), 771e359 (fix: textarea leak)

title: Central user dashboard document / home index for each account
description:
## Problem
Each user currently has no single "home base" document on their account.
When a user signs up, their dashboard is a generic item list. There is no
central index or landing page for their own content that they can shape.

## Proposal
Give every user a special dashboard/index item that:
- Is auto-created at registration (or on first access)
- Lives at /@username (or /@username/home or /@username/index)
- Is the user-controlled landing page for their repo
- Is a regular ListHub item, editable as markdown, so the user can
  customize it however they want (curated links, intro text, featured posts)
- Gets surfaced as the default view when someone visits /@username
- Can optionally be pinned in the sidebar tree so it is always one click away

## Questions
- Default content: empty? seeded with a template listing their recent items?
- If the user already has a lot of items, should the default just be the
  existing folder-tree view? (Current behavior)
- Relationship to the "Personal Index" item I just created
  (/@jacobreal/personal-index) — should that become the canonical home doc?
- Is this the same as a pinned sidebar item? See also listhub-lav (sidebar ticket).

## Related
- The new /@jacobreal/personal-index item — real-world test case
- listhub-lav — persistent sidebar
- listhub-lwh — sub-wiki concept (similar but folder-scoped)

title: Implement community directory — shared git repo at /directory
description: A bare git repo at /repos/directory.git that any authenticated user can clone and push to. Folder/file structure = browsable tree hierarchy at /directory. No DB storage — rendered directly from git. Files to modify: git_backend.py (shared repo routes + _require_any_auth), hooks/post-receive (skip sync for shared), views.py (/directory route), new templates/directory.html, base.html (nav link).

title: LLM Wiki Book — curated index of LLM wikis on ListHub
description: Build a public ListHub list cataloging the LLM wiki ecosystem across 5 senses (Karpathy-style personal wikis, AI-generated encyclopedias, llms.txt/agent-readable, wikis-about-LLMs, human+AI collaborative). First-mover opportunity — Karpathy's gist is days old (2026-04-03), no directory spans all 5 senses, no Moltbook-style archive exists. Full research + plan: research/llm-wiki-research.md and ListHub item /@researchagent/llm-wiki-book-on-listhub-research-plan. List title: 'LLM Wikis' (avoid 'llmwikibook' — reads derivative). Structure: sections by sense, each entry has sense/type/maintainer/status/url/description fields. Ship within 2 weeks while term is fresh.

title: Add Milkdown WYSIWYG editor to ListHub
Public git repo directory at /git/
